message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Objectives</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>To investigate severe COVID-19 risk by occupational group.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Methods</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Baseline UK Biobank data (2006-10) for England were linked to SARS-CoV-2 test results from Public Health England (16 March to 26 July 2020). Included participants were employed or self-employed at baseline, alive and aged less than 65 years in 2020. Poisson regression models adjusted sequentially for baseline demographic, socioeconomic, work-related, health, and lifestyle-related risk factors to assess risk ratios (RRs) for testing positive in hospital or death due to COVID-19 by three occupational classification schemes (including Standard Occupation Classification 2000).</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Of 120,075 participants, 271 had severe COVID-19. Relative to non-essential workers, healthcare workers (RR 7.43, 95% CI:5.52,10.00), social and education workers (RR 1.84, 95% CI:1.21,2.82) and other essential workers (RR=1.60, 95% CI:1.05,2.45) had higher risk of severe COVID-19. Using more detailed groupings, medical support staff (RR 8.70, 95% CI:4.87,15.55), social care (RR 2.46, 95% CI:1.47,4.14) and transport workers (RR= 2.20, 95% CI:1.21,4.00) had highest risk within the broader groups. Compared to white non-essential workers, non-white non-essential workers had a higher risk (RR 3.27, 95% CI: 1.90,5.62) and non-white essential workers had the highest risk (RR 8.34, 95% CI:5.17,13.47). Using SOC2000 major groups, associate professional and technical occupations, personal service occupations and plant and machine operatives had higher risk, compared to managers and senior officials.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusions</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Essential workers have higher risk of severe COVID-19. These findings underscore the need for national and organizational policies and practices that protect and support workers with elevated risk of severe COVID-19.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>
                    <jats:bold>Trial registration</jats:bold>
                    -N/A
                  </jats:title>
                  <jats:list list-type="bullet">
                    <jats:title>What is already known on this topic</jats:title>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>Essential workers have a higher exposure to the SARS-CoV-2 virus due to the nature of their work.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>In comparison to non-essential workers, healthcare workers appear to have a higher risk of SARS-CoV-2 infection.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                  </jats:list>
                  <jats:list list-type="bullet">
                    <jats:title>What this study adds</jats:title>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>Healthcare workers had a more than seven-fold higher risk of severe COVID-19; those working in social care and transport occupations had a two-fold higher risk.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>Adjusting for potential confounding and mediating variables did not fully account for the differences in the observed risk amongst most occupational groups.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>Non-white essential workers had the highest risk of severe COVID-19 infection.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                  </jats:list>
                  <jats:list list-type="bullet">
                    <jats:title>How might this impact on policy or clinical practice in the foreseeable future?</jats:title>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>Our findings reinforce the need for adequate health and safety arrangements and provision of PPE, particularly in the health and social care sectors, and highlight the need for national and organizational policies and practices that protect and support workers with elevated risk of SARS-CoV-2 infection.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                  </jats:list>
                </jats:sec>
title: Occupation and risk of severe COVID-19: prospective cohort study of 120,075 UK Biobank participants
